question:
Which of the following constitute the World Bank? (UPSC Prelims 1995)

1. International Bank for Reconstruction and Development

2. International Finance Corporation

3. International Development Association

4. International Monetary Fund

Choose the correct answer from the codes given below:
option_1: 1, 2 and 3
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 3 and 4
option_4: 1, 2, 3 and 4
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

The term World Bank refers specifically to a group of international financial institutions that provide loans, grants, and technical assistance to developing countries for development projects. The World Bank Group originally consisted of two institutions — the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) and the International Development Association (IDA). Over time, it expanded to include International Finance Corporation (IFC), Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (MIGA), and International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes (ICSID). Among the given options: The IBRD (1) is the main arm of the World Bank that lends to middle-income and creditworthy low-income countries — so it is part of the World Bank. The IFC (2) promotes private sector investment in developing countries — it is also part of the World Bank Group. The IDA (3) provides concessional loans and grants to the poorest nations — it too is part of the World Bank. The IMF (4), however, is not part of the World Bank Group. It is a separate organization that focuses on monetary cooperation, exchange rate stability, and balance of payments support, not development lending. Hence, institutions 1, 2, and 3 together constitute the World Bank, while IMF (4) does not.

question: Corporation tax........... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Is levied and appropriated by the States
option_2: Is levied by the Union and collected and appropriated by the States
option_3: Is levied by the Union and shared by the Union and the States
option_4: Is levied by the Union and belongs to it exclusively
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

  • Corporation tax is levied by the Union Government and belongs to it exclusively.
  • The revenue collected from corporation tax is not shared with the States but can be used by the Union Government for various purposes.
  • Corporation tax is a direct tax levied, collected and appropriated by the Union or the central government.
  • No part of this tax can be assigned to the states.
  • In India, domestic companies are taxed at a rate of 30%.
  • In addition to this, the Income Tax Act levies a surcharge of 7% if the net income ranges from Rs. 1 crore to Rs. 10 crore.

question: The Dinesh Goswami Committee was concerned with............. (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: De-nationalisation of banks
option_2: Electoral reforms
option_3: Steps to put down insurgency in the North- East
option_4: The problem of the Chakmas
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

  • The Dinesh Goswami Committee, formally known as the Committee on Electoral Reforms, was established in 1990 by the Government of India.
  • The committee was tasked with examining and recommending reforms to the electoral system in India.
  • Its recommendations aimed to enhance the democratic process, improve electoral transparency, and address various issues related to elections in the country.

question: Which one of the following is not an instrument of selective credit control in India? (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Regulation of consumer credit
option_2: Rationing of credit
option_3: Margin requirements
option_4: Variable cost reserve ratios
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

  • Variable cost reserve ratios are not an instrument of selective credit control in India.
  • Instead, they are a monetary policy tool used by the Reserve Bank of India to control the money supply in the economy.
  • Variable cost reserve ratios are an expression used to calculate a company’s variable production costs as a percentage of sales.
  • This is done by dividing the variable cost by the total revenues.
  • The variable cost ratio is a calculation of the costs of increasing production compared to the greater revenues that will result from the increase.
  • This allows a company to aim for the optimal balance between increased revenues and increased costs of production.

question: Bank Rate implies the rate of interest.............. (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Paid by the Reserve Bank of India on the Deposits of Commercial Banks
option_2: Charged by Banks on loans and advances
option_3: Payable on Bonds
option_4: At which the Reserve Bank of India discounts the Bills of Exchange
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

  • The Bank rate is the interest rate at which a country’s central bank lends money to commercial banks.
  • It is a key tool for central banks to influence monetary policy, controlling inflation and promoting economic stability.

1. Bill of exchange :

  • It is a written order that requires one party to pay a fixed amount of money to another party. It’s primarily used in international trade.

Here are some characteristics of a bill of exchange:

1. It’s similar to checks and promissory notes.

2. It can be drawn by individuals or banks.

3. It can usually be passed by endorsements.

4. It must be a written document.

5. It must name all relevant parties.

6. It must be addressed from one party to another.

7. It must bear the signature of the party giving it.

  • A common type of bill of exchange is a check. A check is a bill of exchange drawn on a banker and payable on demand.

2. What are Advances?

  • An advance is a short-term credit facility that a bank or financial institution provides to a borrower. Advances are a form of working capital that can be used to cover daily expenses like salaries and wages.

question: Which one of the following is true regarding the Jawahar Rozgar Yojana (JRY)? (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: It was launched during the Prime Ministership of Indira Gandhi
option_2: It aims at creating one million jobs annually
option_3: The target group of JRY are the urban poor living below the poverty line
option_4: Under the scheme 30% of the employment generated is reserved for women
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

What is Jawahar Rozgar Yojana :

  • Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi launched the Jawahar Rozgar Yojana (JRY) on April 1, 1989 in the Seventh Five Year Plan. The JRY was a Centrally Sponsored Scheme.
  • It was established by merging the National Rural Employment Programme and Rural Landless Employment Guarantee Programme.
  • The main objective of this initiative was to provide additional employment in rural areas.
  • Under the Scheme 30% of the employment generated is reserved for Women.

question: The Narasimham Committee for Financial Sector Reforms has suggested reduction in.......... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: SLR and CRR
option_2: SLR, CRR and Priority Sector Financing
option_3: SLR and Financing to capital goods sector.
option_4: CRR, Priority Sector Financing and Financing to capital goods sector
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

The Narasimham Committee for Financial Sector Reforms suggested reducing the following:

  • SLR, CRR, and Priority Sector Financing: The committee recommended reducing these from 40% to 10%.
  • The committee also redefined the priority sector to include small businesses, marginal farmers, and other sectors.

The Narasimham Committee :

  • It was established in 1991 by former RBI Governor M. Narasimham to examine all aspects of India’s financial sector.
  • The committee is also known as the Committee on Banking Sector Reforms.

option_1: Rice

option_3: Rubber
option_4: Wheat
solution_text:

question:
Which of the following benefits are likely to accrue to India from the World Trade Organisation? (UPSC Prelims 1995)

1. India’s share in the world trade is to go up from the present 600 million US dollars to 5 billion US dollars by 2000 AD.

2. It will help boost exports of agricultural commodities from India.

3. India’s share in the world trade is likely to triple by the year 2000 A.D.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1, 2 and 3
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 1 and 3
option_4: 2 and 3
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

  • India has experienced several positive impacts as a result of its involvement in the World Trade Organization (WTO).

Some of these include:

1. Enhanced Market Access: WTO agreements have improved India’s access to global markets.

2. Trade Facilitation: Streamlined trade procedures under WTO have reduced barriers and increased efficiency.

3. Rules-Based System: WTO provides a structured platform for dispute resolution, protecting India’s interests.

4. Foreign Direct Investment: Participation in WTO has attracted FDI, contributing to economic growth.

5. Technology Transfer: Global trade facilitated by WTO has led to technology transfer, fostering innovation.

6. Agricultural Reforms: WTO negotiations addressed agricultural subsidies, benefiting Indian farmers.

7. Textiles and Clothing Opportunities: Phasing out of the MFA opened up opportunities in the global textiles and clothing market.

8. Services Sector Growth: WTO agreements have positively impacted India’s services sector.

Hence, The Correct Answer is (D) 2 and 3.

question: One of the reasons for occupational structure remaining more or less the same over the years has been that............ (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: productivity in agriculture has been high enough to induce people to stay with agriculture.
option_2: ceilings on land holdings have enabled more people to own land and hence their preference to stay with agriculture.
option_3: people are largely unaware of the significance of transition from agriculture to industry.
option_4: for economic development investment pattern has been directed towards capital-intensive industries.
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

Here are some other reasons for India’s occupational structure remaining more or less the same over the years:

  • Productivity in agriculture: High enough to encourage people to stay with agriculture.
  • Land holdings: Ceilings have enabled more people to own land and prefer to stay with agriculture.
  • Awareness: People are largely unaware of the significance of transition from agriculture to industry
  • Occupational structure : It is distribution of occupations in society, defined by skill level, economic function, or social rank. Various factors influence the occupational structure, including the economy’s structure, technology and bureaucracy, the labor market, and status and prestige.

question: The largest source of financing the public sector outlay of the Eighth Five-Year Plan comes from.............. (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: balance from current revenue
option_2: contribution of public enterprises
option_3: government borrowings
option_4: deficit financing
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

  • Deficit financing is a policy that involves borrowing funds from the public or central bank to fill the gap between government revenue and expenditure.
  • The government resorts to deficit financing when it is unable to cover its total expenditure from other sources of revenue.
  • The eighth five year plan from 1992–1997 was executed by the government of P.V. Narasimha Rao. The plan focused on human resources, such as education, employment, and public health.
  • The Eighth Five-Year Plan aimed to promote the growth of the country by creating a balance between the government’s spending and revenue.
  • The government spends more money than it collects as revenue, and the difference is made up by borrowing from the Reserve Bank of India by issuing promissory notes.

question: The main reason for low growth rate in India, in spite of high rate of savings and capital formation is............. (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: high birth rate
option_2: low level of foreign aid
option_3: low capital / output ratio
option_4: high capital / output ratio
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

  • The capital-output ratio (COR) is the relationship between investment and resulting output over time.
  • It measures the percentage increase in capital formation required to obtain a percentage increase in GDP.
  • The capital-output ratio is the number of units of capital required to produce one unit of output over a given period of time.
  • When the capital-output ratio is high, it means that a large amount of capital is required to produce a given amount of output.
  • In this situation, an increase in capital formation may not result in a significant increase in output.

question:
Consider the following : (UPSC Prelims 1995)

1. Industrial Finance Corporation of India

2. Industrial Credit and Investment Corporation of India

3. Industrial Development Bank of India

4. Unit Trust of India

The correct sequence in which the above were established is
option_1: 1, 2, 4, 3
option_2: 1, 3, 2, 4
option_3: 4, 3, 2, 1
option_4: 1, 4, 3, 2
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

Industrial Finance Corporation of India :

  • It was the first financial institution established in 1948 to provide long-term finance to the industrial sector in India. The headquarter of (IFCI) is located at Delhi. 

The Industrial Credit and Investment Corporation of India (ICICI):

  • It is a financial institution that was established in 1955 to develop small and medium-sized private sector industries. ICICI is governed by the Indian Company Act. In 1994, ICICI joined the financial sector by establishing ICICI Bank as its financial branch. In 1998, ICICI Bank became the first bank in India to offer internet banking. ICICI Bank’s headquarter are located at Mumbai. 

The Industrial Development Bank of India (IDBI) :

  • It is a private sector bank that provides financial and banking services. It was established in 1964 by the Government of India to provide financial and credit facilities for the growth of Indian industries. IDBI’s headquarters are in Mumbai.

The Unit Trust of India (UTI):

  • It was established on February 1, 1964, under the Unit Trust of India Act of 1963. It is a statutory public sector investment institution.
  • The UTI’s primary objective is to encourage and mobilize community savings and channel them into productive corporate investment. In 1964, UTI launched its first investment scheme, Unit Scheme-1964 (US-64), which is still its largest.
  • UTI Mutual Fund’s corporate office is located in the Mumbai.

question: The new Exim Policy announced in 1992, is for a period of............... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: 3 years
option_2: 4 years
option_3: 7 years
option_4: 5 years
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

  • The new Exim Policy announced in 1992, is for period of 5 years.
  • The Export-Import (EXIM) Policy of India is a set of guidelines that govern the import and export of goods and services in and out of the country. The policy is also known as the Foreign Trade Policy (FTP). The main objective of the EXIM Policy is to regulate and develop India’s foreign trade.

question: Which one of the following is correct regarding stabilization and structural adjustment as two components of the new economic policy adopted in India? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Stabilization is a gradual, multi-step process while structural adjustment is a quick adaptation process
option_2: Structural adjustment is a gradual, multi-step process, while stabilization is a quick adaptation process
option_3: Stabilization and structural adjustment are very similar and complementary policies. It is difficult to separate one from the other
option_4: Stabilization mainly deals with a set of policies which are to be implemented by the Central Government while structural adjustment is to be set in motion by the State Governments
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

  • The New Economic Policy (NEP) was adopted in 1991 based on the Rao Manmohan model. The NEP includes both stabilization and structural adjustment measures.
  • Stabilization and Structural adjustment are similar and complementary policies.
  • Stabilization is mainly implemented by the Central Government, while structural adjustment is implemented by the State Governments.

question: A redistribution of income in a country can be best brought about through combined with.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: progressive taxation combined with progressive expenditure.
option_2: progressive taxation combined with regressive expenditure
option_3: regressive taxation combined with regressive expenditure
option_4: regressive taxation combined with progressive expenditure
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • Progressive taxation combined with progressive expenditure is the best way to redistribute income in a country.
  • Progressive taxation is a tax system where people with higher incomes pay a higher percentage of their income in taxes.
  • Progressive expenditure is when the government supports the poor with policies like welfare programs and tax credits.
  • This system ensures that the wealthy pay a higher tax rate, and the revenue is used to fund social welfare programs that help the poor.
  • Some critics of progressive taxes believe they are a disincentive to success and unfairly punish the wealthy, upper class, and middle class.

question: One of the important goals of the economic liberalisation policy is to achieve full convertibility of the Indian rupee. This is being advocated because.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: convertibility of the rupee will stabilize its exchange value against major currencies of the world
option_2: it will attract more foreign capital inflow in India
option_3: it will help promote exports
option_4: it will help India secure loans from the world financial markets at attractive terms
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • Convertibility of the rupee will stabilize its exchange value against major currencies of the world.
  • Economic liberalization refers to the opening up of an economy to global trade and investment, reducing government regulations and barriers to international economic transactions.

What is Convertibility of the rupee?

  • Convertibility of the rupee refers to the ability to freely exchange the Indian rupee for other major currencies or gold without any restrictions.
  • Convertibility can be either full or partial, and it is influenced by a country’s economic policies, foreign exchange reserves, and overall economic stability.
  • India has moved towards greater rupee convertibility over the years, allowing more flexibility in foreign exchange transactions.

question:
Assertion (A) : An important policy instrument of economic liberalization is reduction in import duties on capital goods.

Reason (R) : Reduction in import duties would help the local entrepreneurs to improve technology to face the global markets.

In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true and R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • Reducing import duties on capital goods is an important policy instrument of economic liberalization.
  • Reducing import duties can help local entrepreneurs improve technology to compete in global markets.
  • Liberalization is a broad term that refers to the practice of making laws, systems, or opinions less severe. In economics, it refers to the removal or reduction of restrictions placed upon economic activity.
  • Liberalization can increase growth rates in a short period of time. This can also result in higher imports than exports.

question:
Which of the following were the aims behind the setting up of the World Trade Organization (WTO)? (UPSC Prelims 1996)

1. Promotion of free trade and resource flows across countries.

2. Protection of intellectual property rights.

3. Managing balanced trade between different countries.

4. Promotion of trade between the former East Block countries and the western world.

Select the correct answer by using the codes given. below:
option_1: 1, 2, 3 and 4
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1 and 4
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • The World Trade Organization (WTO) is a global organization that regulates and facilitates international trade.
  • It’s made up of 164 member countries and its goal is to ensure that trade flows as smoothly and predictably as possible.
  • The WTO’s trading system is half a century older, with the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) providing the rules since 1948.
  • The WTO began on January 1, 1995.

The WTO deals with:

1. Agriculture

2. Textiles and clothing

3. Banking

4. Telecommunications

5. Government purchases

6. Industrial standards and product safety

7. Food sanitation regulations

8. Intellectual property

The WTO’s principles include:

1. Each member has a single voting right.

2. Its members enjoy international privilege

3. It administers a unified package of an agreement to which all members are committed.

  • India has been a member of the World Trade Organization (WTO) since January 1, 1995.
  • India was also a member of the WTO’s predecessor, the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT), since July 1948.

question: The emerging trading blocs in the world, such as NAFTA, ASEAN and the like, are expected to............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Act as constrictions in free trade across the world
option_2: Promote free trade on the lines laid down by the WTO
option_3: Permit transfer of technology between member countries
option_4: Promote trade in agricultural commodities between countries of the North and South
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

  • Emerging trading blocs, like NAFTA and ASEAN, are expected to promote free trade along the lines of the WTO.
  • Trade blocs are intergovernmental agreements that reduce or eliminate trade barriers between participating states. They can be stand-alone agreements or part of a regional organization.
  • Trading blocs can help promote good international relationships between members. They also increase consumer surplus by lowering prices and increasing the choice of goods and services.

Here are some examples of trading blocs:

1. North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) : ∙

  • 1A trade bloc between Canada, Mexico, and the United States that came into force in 1994. NAFTA is one of the world’s largest trade blocs by gross domestic product.

2. Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) : ∙

  • A regional grouping of ten countries that aims to promote economic and security cooperation.
  •  It was established on August 8, 1967, in Bangkok, Thailand.
  •  The five founding member countries of ASEAN are Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, and Thailand.
  •  It works to promote economic and cultural exchange, maintain peace and stability, and establish relationships with foreign powers. ∙ India is not a member of ASEAN.

question: The Eighth Five-Year Plan is different from the earlier ones. The critical difference lies in the fact that............ (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: it has a considerably larger outlay compared to the earlier plans
option_2: it has a major thrust on agricultural and rural development
option_3: considerable emphasis is placed on infrastructure growth
option_4: industrial licensing has been abolished.
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • The Eighth Five-Year Plan (1992-1997) differs from earlier plans because it has a significantly larger outlay.
  • The increased outlay demonstrates a higher level of financial commitment towards achieving the plan’s goals.
  • The Eighth Plan promoted the modernization of industries. India became a member of the World Trade Organization on January 1, 1995.

question: Hawala transactions relate to payments............ (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Received in rupees against overseas currencies and vice versa without going through the official channels
option_2: Received for sale/transfer of shares without going through the established stock exchanges
option_3: Received as commission for services rendered to overseas investors/ buyers/sellers in assisting them to get over the red tape and/or in getting preferential treatment
option_4: Made to political parties or to individuals for meeting election expenses
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • The hawala system is an alternative money transfer system that originated in South Asia in the 8th century.
  • Hawala is an informal and alternative remittance system that operates outside of traditional banking channels.
  • It involves the transfer of money or value between two parties without the actual physical movement of funds.
  • Hawala transactions are based on trust and rely on a network of brokers, known as hawaladars, who facilitate the transfer of funds between different locations.
  • While it can be used for legitimate purposes, hawala has also been associated with illicit activities due to its potential for anonymity.

question:
Assertion (A) : Though India’s national income has gone up several fold since 1947, there has been no marked improvement in the per capita income level. (UPSC Prelims 1996)

Reason (R) : Sizeable proportion of the population of India is still living below the poverty line.

In the context of the above two statements which one of the following is correct?
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R. are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R. is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
  • India’s national income has increased several times since 1947, but there has not been a significant improvement in per capita income.

Some reasons for this include:

1. Poverty

2. Rapid population growth

3. Lack of education, healthcare, and employment opportunities

Other reasons for India’s low national income include:

1. High population growth rate

2. Excessive dependence on agriculture

3. Occupational structure

4. Low level of technology and its poor adoption

5. Poor industrial development

6. Poor development of infrastructural facilities.

What is National Income?

  • National income refers to the total monetary value of all goods and services produced within a country’s borders over a specific period.
  • It includes factors like wages, profits, and taxes, providing a measure of a nation’s economic performance.
  • India’s gross national income in 2023 was estimated to be over 267 trillion Indian rupees.

question:
In India, rural incomes are generally lower than the urban incomes. Which of the following reasons account for this? (UPSC Prelims 1996)

1. A large number of farmers are illiterate and know little about scientific agriculture.

2. Prices of primary products are lower than those of manufactured products.

3. Investment in agriculture has been low when compared to investment in industry.

Select the correct answer by using the codes given. below:
option_1: 1, 2 and 3
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 1 and 3
option_4: 2 and 3
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

All of the following reasons contribute to lower rural incomes in India than urban incomes:

1. Low prices of primary products: Primary products, like agriculturalcommodities, are often priced lower than manufactured products. This can affect rural farmers’ income, as they depend on selling their produce for a living.

2. Low investment in agriculture: Investment in agriculture is low compared to investment in industry.

3. Lack of knowledge and practices in agriculture: Many farmers in rural areas are illiterate and have limited knowledge of modern agricultural practices. They often rely on traditional farming methods, which may not be as productive or profitable.

  • According to a 2023, Economic Survey, 65% of India’s population lives in rural areas, and 47% of the population depends on agriculture for their livelihood.

question:
Consider the following items imported by India............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)

1. Capital goods

2. Petroleum

3. Pearls and precious stones

4. Chemicals

5. Iron and Steel

The correct sequence of the decreasing order of these items (as per 94-95 figures), in terms of value is
option_1: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
option_2: 1, 2, 4, 3, 5
option_3: 2, 1, 3, 4, 5
option_4: 2, 1, 4, 5, 3
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (D)

The Correct Answer is (D) 2 1 4 5 3.

  • Petroleum tends to have the highest value, followed by capital goods, chemicals, iron and steel, and then pearls and precious stones.

Petroleum :

  • India is a net exporter of petroleum products, but it also imports a significant amount of crude oil and natural gas.

Capital Goods :

  • In 2021, India was the world’s 14th largest exporter and 11th largest importer of capital goods.

Chemicals :

  • India ranks 11th in the world for chemical exports and 6th for chemical imports. India is the sixth largest chemical producer in the world and the third largest in Asia.

Iron and Steel :

  • India is the world’s third largest exporter of iron and steel. India is also the world’s second largest producer of crude steel. India is the world’s third largest consumer of steel. However, India became a net importer of steel in October 2023.

Pearls and Precious Stones :

  • India is the sixth largest importer of precious stones in the world.

question: “They are fantastically diverse. They speak hundreds of languages and dialects. They comprise scores of ethnic groups. They include highly industrialised economies and up-coming economies. They span half the surface of the earth and are home to two-fifths of the world’s population.” The group of countries referred to here belongs to.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: SAPTA
option_2: APEC
option_3: EC
option_4: CIC
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

The Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) :

  • It was established in 1989 to promote economic growth and prosperity in the Asia-Pacific region. APEC is a regional economic forum that includes 21 member countries.

The founding members of APEC were:

  • Australia, Brunei Darussalam, Canada, Indonesia, Japan, Korea, Malaysia, NewZealand, The Philippines, Singapore, Thailand. India is not a member of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC). However, India has been an observer since the late 1990s.
  • The 2023 Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) Leaders’ Summit was held in San Francisco, California from November 11–17, 2023. The theme of the summit was “Creating a Resilient and Sustainable Future for All”.

SAPTA :

  • South Asian Preferential Trading Arrangement. It was signed in 1993 by the seven member countries of the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC)
  • Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka. The agreement’s goal was to promote trade liberalization among the member countries. The headquarters of the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC) is located at Kathmandu, Nepal.
  • In 2004, the South Asian Free Trade Area (SAFTA) was launched as an improved version of SAPTA. SAFTA created a free-trade area for 1.6 billion people in Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, the Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka.

question: Which one of the following sets of commodities are exported to India by arid and semi-arid countries in the Middle East? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Raw wool and carpets
option_2: Fruits and palm oil
option_3: Precious stones and pearls
option_4: Perfume and coffee
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

Palm Oil :

  • Palm oil is a vegetable oil that comes from the fruit of oil palms. It’s used in food manufacturing, cosmetics, and as biofuel.
  • As per latest Reports, Palm oil accounted for about 36% of global oils produced fromoil crops. Andhra Pradesh is the largest producer of palm oil in India

Wool :

  • India ranks 6th amongst clean wool producer countries and 9th amongst greasy wool producers. Indian wool is almost exclusively of broader micron and used in manufacturing of carpets and rugs.
  • India has 3rd largest sheep population in the world. Rajasthan is India’s largest wool producer, contributing over 30% of the country’s wool. Rajasthan is known for its high-quality carpet wool, including Chokla and Magra wool.

  • In 2021, India was the fourth largest exporter of precious stones and the 20th largest exporter of pearls in the world. Odisha is the leading producer of pearls in India, Madhya Pradesh is the leading producer of diamonds in India, accounting for 32% of the country’s production, The Karur-Kangayam and Hole-Narsipur belts in Tamil Nadu and Karnataka are known for their gemstones.

question: The economic and monetary union of 15 European Countries is proposed to be made by 1999. But the currencies of two countries, Franc, have already the same value and circulate freely in both the countries. The countries are........... (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: France and Switzerland
option_2: Switzerland and Luxembourg
option_3: Luxembourg and Belgium
option_4: France and Belgium
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (C)

The question refers to a time before the creation of the Euro, when several European countries had their own national currencies. Among these, Belgium and Luxembourg had a special monetary arrangement known as the Belgium–Luxembourg Economic Union (BLEU), established in 1921. Under this agreement, the Belgian franc and the Luxembourg franc were kept at par value (i.e., equal exchange rate) and both currencies were freely interchangeable and circulated in both countries. This monetary union functioned successfully for decades before both countries later joined the Eurozone in 1999, adopting the euro as their common currency. Hence, the two countries whose currencies (francs) had the same value and circulated freely were Luxembourg and Belgium.

question:
The sum of which of the following constitutes Broad Money in India? (UPSC Prelims 1997)

1. Currency with the Public

2. Demand deposits with banks

3. Time deposits with banks

4. Other deposits with RBI

Choose the correct answer using the codes given. below:
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 1, 2 and 3
option_3: 1, 2, 3 and 4
option_4: 1, 2 and 4
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (C)

In India, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) defines various monetary aggregates such as M1, M2, M3, and M4, which measure the money supply at different levels of liquidity. The concept of Broad Money corresponds to M3, which is the most commonly used measure of money supply in India. According to the RBI’s definition, M3 = Currency with the Public + Demand Deposits with Banks + Time Deposits with Banks + Other Deposits with RBI. Currency with the Public : This includes all notes and coins held by the public (excluding the cash held by banks). Demand Deposits with Banks : These are deposits that can be withdrawn on demand, such as savings and current accounts. Time Deposits with Banks : These are deposits with a fixed maturity period, such as fixed deposits and recurring deposits. Other Deposits with RBI : These are deposits maintained by non-government financial institutions, foreign central banks, and international agencies with the RBI. Since Broad Money (M3) includes all four components — currency with the public, demand deposits, time deposits, and other deposits with the RBI — all the given items are part of it. Thus, the correct answer is (c) 1, 2, 3 and 4.

List II

of women

equality

question:
Asscertion (A) : The United States of America has threatened to ask the World Trade Organisation (WTO) to apply sanctions the developing countries for the non-observance of ILO conventions. (UPSC Prelims 1998)

Reason (R) : The United States of America itself has adopted and implemented those ILO conventions
option_1: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

The Assertion (A) is true — the United States has, at various points, urged the WTO to link international trade privileges with labour standards laid down by the International Labour Organization (ILO). The U.S. has argued that developing countries gain unfair trade advantages by not adhering to core labour standards such as the prohibition of child labour, freedom of association, and the right to collective bargaining. Hence, it has pushed for trade sanctions or restrictions through WTO mechanisms to compel compliance with ILO conventions. However, the Reason (R) is false. The United States itself has not ratified several key ILO conventions, including those related to freedom of association and collective bargaining — mainly due to domestic legal and constitutional reasons. Therefore, although the U.S. advocates for labour standards globally, it does not fully implement or legally adopt all of those conventions at home. Thus, while the assertion correctly states the U.S. position toward developing countries, the reason wrongly assumes that the U.S. has implemented all ILO conventions. Thus, A is true, but R is false.

question: The banks are required to maintain a certain ratio between their cash in hand and total assets. This is called............. (UPSC Prelims 1998)

Central bank reserves :

Central Liquid Reserve :

  • Central banks use reserve requirements to set the minimum amount of liquid assets that a commercial bank must hold.

  • India’s fiscal year aligns with the country’s agricultural cycle. Crop sowing begins in April, and the harvest takes place between October and March.

question: ‘Eco mark’ is given to the Indian products that are........... (UPSC Prelims 1998)

question: Which one of the following is the correct sequence of decreasing order of the given currencies in terms of their value in Indian Rupees............ (UPSC Prelims 1998)

question: Capital Account Convertibility of the Indian Rupee implies............. (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: that the Indian Rupee can be exchanged by the authorised dealers for travel
option_2: that the Indian Rupee can be exchanged for any major currency for the purpose of trade in goods and services
option_3: that the Indian Rupee can be exchanged for any major currency for the purpose of trading financial assets
option_4: None of the above
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (C)

  • Capital account convertibility (CAC) is a country’s ability to convert its currency into foreign exchange without restrictions.
  • It also refers to the ability to convert local financial assets into foreign financial assets at market-determined exchange rates.
  • For example, CAC allows Indians to convert their rupees into dollars or euros without restrictions.
  • It also means that there are no restrictions on the amount of rupees that can be converted into foreign currency to acquire foreign assets.

option_4: gold price has gone up 3.3 times
solution_text:

question: Economic Survey in India is published officially, every year by the............ (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Reserve Bank of India
option_2: Planning Commission of India
option_3: Ministry of Finance, Government of India
option_4: Ministry of Industries, Government of India
solution_text:

  • The Ministry of Finance, Government of India publishes the Economic Survey of India every year. The Economics Division of the Department of Economic Affairs, which is part of the Finance Ministry, prepares the Economic Survey.
  • The Economic Survey is a comprehensive document that reviews the development of the Indian economy over the previous 12 months. The Department of Economic Affairs presents the Survey in Parliament every year, just before the Union Budget.
  • The practice of tabling the economic survey began in the fiscal year 1950-51. Previously, it was included in the budget documents. In the 1960s, it was separated as a separate exercise.

question: Persons below the poverty line in India are classified as such based on whether............. (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: they are entitled to a minimum prescribed food basket
option_2: they get work for a prescribed minimum number of days in a year
option_3: they belong to agricultural labourer household and the scheduled caste/ tribe social group
option_4: their daily wages fall below the prescribed minimum wages
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • In India, people are classified as below the poverty line if they can’t afford a minimum prescribed food basket.
  • This means that if they can’t afford basic needs, especially food, they fall below the poverty line.
  • The poverty line is determined by calculating the minimum level of food, clothing, footwear, fuel and light, educational and medical requirements, and more. These physical quantities are then multiplied by their prices in rupees to arrive at the poverty line.

What is Multidimensional Poverty Index?

  • The Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) is a metric that measures acute poverty in over 100 developing countries. The MPI was developed in 2010 by the Oxford Poverty & Human Development Initiative (OPHI) and the United Nations Development Programme.

The MPI measures deprivations in health, education, and living standards by looking at 10 indicators:

1. Nutrition

2. Child mortality

3. Years of schooling

4. School attendance

5. Access to cooking fuel

6. Sanitation

7. Drinking water

8. Electricity

9. Housing

10. Ownership of assets

  • The MPI is published by the UNDP’s Human Development Report Office. It provides a holistic view of poverty by capturing the severe deprivations each person faces concurrently in education, health, and living standards.
  • The MPI uses a scale from 0 to 1, with 0 being the best and 1 being the worst (poorest). According to Statista, Chad had the highest prevalence of multidimensional poverty in the world based on surveys from 2017 to 2022.

question: From the balance sheet of a company, it is possible to............. (UPSC Prelims 1999)

option_3: Determine the size and composition of the assets and liabilities of the company

  • A balance sheet is a financial snapshot of a company, listing its assets, liabilities, and equity ownership at a specific date.
  • From the Balance sheet of a company, it is possible to determine the size and composition of the assets and liabilities of the company.

question: Which one of the following statements regarding the levying, collecting and distribution of Income Tax is correct? (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: The Union levies, collects and distributes the proceeds of income tax between itself and the States
option_2: The Union levies, collects and keeps all the proceeds of income-tax to itself
option_3: The Union levies and collects the tax but all the proceeds are distributed among the States
option_4: Only the surcharge levied on income tax is shared between the Union and the States
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • The correct statement regarding the levying, collecting, and distribution of income tax is:
  • The Union levies, collects, and distributes the proceeds of income-tax between itself and the States.
  • The Indian Constitution has divided certain powers between the Union and States, which includes taxation powers.
  • As per article 270 of the constitution, all incomes other than agricultural income are liable to be taxed.

Income tax:

  • It is a type of tax that governments impose on income generated by businesses and individuals within their jurisdiction. Income taxes are a source of revenue for governments.
  • They are used to fund public services, pay government obligations, and provide goods for citizens.

question:

question: Tourism industry in India is quite small compared to many other countries in terms of India’s potential and size. Which one of the following statements is correct in this regard? (UPSC Prelims 1999)

question:
Consider the following statements: Industrial development in India, to an extent, is constrained by........... (UPSC Prelims 1999)

1. Lack of adequate entrepreneurship and leadership in business.

2. Lack of savings to invest.

3. Lack of technology, skills and infrastructure.

option_2: 1, 3 and 4
option_3: 2, 3 and 4

Industrial development in India is constrained by a number of factors, including :

  2. Capital formation: A poor rate of capital formation is considered a major constraint on India’s industrial growth.

question: The planning process in the industrial sector in India has assumed a relatively less important position in the nineties as compared to that in the earlier period. Which one of the following is true in this regard? (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: With the advent of liberalization, industrial investments/development have largely been placed within the domain of private and multinational sectors
option_2: With markets assuming a central place, the role of central planning in many sectors has been rendered redundant

  • The role of central planning has been rendered redundant due to markets assuming a central place.
  • This is not entirely true. Despite market liberalization, the role of central planning, especially in a country like India, still has significance in various sectors like infrastructure, public welfare, etc.
  • Therefore, option 2 is the correct answer

question: The Employment Assurance Scheme envisages financial assistance to rural areas for guaranteeing employment to at least............ (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: 50 per cent of the men and women seeking jobs in rural areas.

option_3: one man and one woman in a rural family living below the poverty line

solution_text:

  • The Employment Assurance Scheme (EAS) provides financial assistance to rural areas to guarantee employment to at least one able-bodied person in poor families.
  • The EAS is open to all adult rural poor who are in need of wage employment.
  • The EAS’s primary objective is to provide gainful employment, particularly during the lean agricultural season.

question: The farmers are provided credit from a number of sources for their short and long- term needs. The main sources of credit to the farmers include............. (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: the Primary Agricultural Cooperative Societies, commercial banks, RRBs and private moneylenders
option_2: the NABARD, RBI, commercial banks and private moneylenders
option_3: the District Central Cooperative Banks (DCCB), the lead banks, IRDP and JRY
option_4: the Large-Scale Multi-purpose Adivasis Programme, DCCB, IFFCO anc commercial banks
solution_text:

The main sources of credit for farmers include:

1. Institutional sources: Co-operatives, commercial banks, and the RBI and NABARD

2. Non-institutional sources: Moneylenders, traders and commission agents, relatives, and landlords

  • NABARD, the National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development, is a development bank that provides and regulates credit for the development of agriculture and other rural activities.
  • Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) help increase financial inclusion in rural areas by providing basic banking services, such as savings and loan accounts.

question:
Consider the following statements: Small- scale industries are, in most cases, not as efficient and competitive as the large scale ones. Yet the Government provides preferential treatment and reservations in a range of products to the small firms because small-scale industries.............. (UPSC Prelims 1999)

1. provide higher employment on a per unit capital deployment basis.

2. promote a regional dispersion of industries and economic activities.

3. have performed better in export of manufactured products than the large scale ones.

4. provide jobs to low-skill workers, who otherwise may not find employment avenues elsewhere.

Which of the above statements are correct?
option_1: 1 and 4
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 3 and 4
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

Small-scale industries are often less efficient than large-scale industries in terms of production efficiency and competitiveness. However, governments support them because they offer several benefits: Higher employment per unit capital: Small-scale industries tend to use more labor relative to capital compared to large-scale industries. This means they create more jobs for a given investment. Regional dispersion: By establishing in smaller towns and regions, SSIs promote balanced economic development across the country. This helps reduce regional inequalities. Employment for low-skill workers: Small-scale industries often require less skilled labor, providing employment opportunities for individuals who may not have the skills for large-scale industries. Why other options are incorrect: Statement 3: While small-scale industries contribute to exports, large-scale industries generally perform better in terms of export volumes. Statement 4: Although some small-scale industries might provide jobs for low-skill workers, this is not the primary reason for government support. The focus is more on promoting regional development and generating overall employment.

question: Which one of the following is the objective of National Renewal Fund? (UPSC Prelims 1999)

option_2: ITO develop the core sector of the economy
option_3: For the development of infrastructure such as energy, transport, communications and irrigation
option_4: For human resource development such as full literacy, employment, population control, housing and drinking water
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • The National Renewal Fund (NRF) was established in 1992 by the Government of India. The fund was created to protect the interests of workers affected by industrial restructuring.

question:
The product life cycle from inception to demise is shown in the graph. Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists: (UPSC Prelims 1999)

 

List I (Stage)

List II (Zone)

1.

Product Development

E

2.

Maturity

D

3.

Growth

B

4.

Introduction

C

Codes : A B C D
option_1: 1 4 2 3
option_2: 1 4 3 2
option_3: 4 1 2 3
option_4: 4 1 3 2
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (B)

Product life-cycle stages progress in time from development (no sales) to introduction (sales start slowly), then to growth (sales and profits rise rapidly), and finally to maturity (sales peak and growth slows). On the graph the leftmost zone (where sales are zero and profits negative) is Product Development — so 1 comes first. Next zone shows sales beginning and profits still low/negative, which is the Introduction stage — that’s 4. After introduction sales climb steeply and profits improve, which is the Growth stage — that’s 3. The zone where sales have peaked and begun stabilizing (competition, price pressure, slower growth) is Maturity — that’s 2. Therefore the correct mapping is 1 → Development, 4 → Introduction, 3 → Growth, 2 → Maturity, giving the code 1432.

question: Since 1980, the share of the tertiary sector in the total GDP of India has............ (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Shown an increasing trend
option_2: Shown a decreasing trend
option_3: Remained constant
option_4: Been fluctuating
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • Since 1980, the share of the tertiary sector in the total GDP of India has shown an increasing trend.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP) :

  • It is a measure of the total economic output of a country, including the value of goods and services produced within its borders over a specific time period, usually a year.
  • As of December 2023, India’s GDP was $3.73 trillion. India is the world’s fifth largest economy by nominal GDP. It is also the third-largest by purchasing power parity (PPP).

What is Tertiary Sector?

  • The tertiary sector, also known as the service sector, is the third tier of the three-sector economy. It involves providing services to other businesses and consumers.
  • The tertiary sector includes a wide range of businesses, such as: Financial institutions, Schools, Hotels, Restaurants.

Examples of tertiary activities include:

  • Transport, Communication, Trade, Health, Education, Administration, Banking, Insurance, Tourism.
  • The tertiary sector supports the primary and secondary sectors through various services. It also provides employment for poor and unskilled workers.

question:
Assertion (A) : Devaluation of a currency may promote export. (UPSC Prelims 1999)

Reason (R) : Price of the country’s products in the international market may fall due to devaluation.
option_1: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • The correct answer is “Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.” Devaluation is an economic policy that involves a country’s government weakening the value of its currency.

Here are some effects of currency devaluation on exports:

1. Exports become cheaper: A devalued currency makes a country’s exports cheaper and more competitive in the global market.

2. Imports become more expensive: A devalued currency means morelocal currency is needed to purchase imports.

3. Exporters earn more money: Exporters get more local currency when they convert the export proceeds.

4. Importers lose money: Importers lose money as imports become more expensive.

5. Trade deficits shrink: Devaluation can help shrink trade deficits.

6. Struggling economies are boosted: Devaluation can boost a country’s struggling economy by increasing exports and reducing imports.

question: The population growth rate in Kerala is the lowest among major Indian states. Which one of the following is the most widely accepted reason for this? (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Kerala has made the highest investment in family planning
option_2: Kerala has the highest literacy rate in India.
option_3: Kerala has invested heavily in promoting literacy and public health and placed high priority on social policies
option_4: The population pyramid in Kerala has relatively fewer women in the reproductive age- group
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (C)

  • The most widely accepted reason for Kerala’s low population growth rate is its high literacy rate, especially among women.
  • Kerala has the highest literacy rate among major Indian states at 94 %.
  • Low birth rates & Late births are other reasons for Kerala’s low population growth.

question: Transchart is the........... (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: chartering wing of the Ministry of Shipping Transport
option_2: container service established by Indian Railways
option_3: training institute of maritime studies and research
option_4: passenger insurance scheme of Indian
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A)

  • Transchart is a government chartering organization that was established in 1960 to arrange shipping for government controlled cargoes. It also handles trading through ships by private sectors. Transchart is a centralized wing of the shipping ministry that sets freight rates.

question:
Assertion (A) : Fiscal deficit is greater than budgetary deficit. (UPSC Prelims 1999)

Reason (R) : Fiscal deficit is the borrowings from the Reserve Bank of India plus other liabilities of the Government to meet its expenditure.
option_1: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (A) The correct answer is “Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.” In the Union Budget, fiscal deficit is the difference between the government’s total revenue and total expenditure. It’s calculated as Total Expenditure minus Total Revenue. Fiscal deficit : It occurs when the government spends more than it earns. The government can borrow money from the reserve bank to fund its expenditures. The fiscal deficit is calculated in absolute terms and as a percentage of the country’s gross domestic product (GDP). India’s current fiscal deficit is 87 trillion. The current fiscal deficit is 6.5 percent of GDP. Budgetary Deficit : It occurs when a government’s expenditures exceed its revenues over a specific period, usually a fiscal year.It indicates that the government is spending more money than it is earning through taxes, fees, and other revenue streams.

question:
Assertion (A) : Information technology is fast becoming a very important field of activity in India. (UPSC Prelims 1999)

Reason (R) : Software is one of the major exports of the country and India has a very strong base in hardware.
option_1: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (C)

The Assertion (A) is true. Information Technology (IT) has indeed become one of the most important and fastest-growing sectors in India. Since the 1990s, the Indian IT industry has contributed significantly to GDP growth, employment generation, and exports. Cities such as Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, and Gurugram have emerged as global IT hubs, making IT a vital field of activity in India’s economy. However, the Reason (R) is partly incorrect. It is true that software forms one of India’s major exports, as the country is globally recognized for its strength in software services, IT consulting, and outsourcing. But the statement that India has a very strong base in hardware is false. India’s IT success is largely software-oriented, while the hardware manufacturing base remains relatively weak, with most hardware components being imported. Therefore, while Assertion (A) is true, the Reason (R) is false due to the incorrect claim about India’s hardware base.

question:
Assertion (A) : According to statistics, more female children, are born each year than male children in India. (UPSC Prelims 1999)

Reason (R) : In India, the death rate of a male child is higher than that of the female child.
option_1: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A.
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (C) In India the sex ratio of female is lower than male because female foeticide is common across India. Sex ratio is the number of females per 1,000 males in a given population. A balanced sex ratio is 1:1, or 1,000 females for every 1,000 males. In 2011, India’s sex ratio was 940 females per 1,000 males, which was an increase from 933 in 2001.Haryana has the lowest sex ratio in India, with 877 females per 1,000 males. Kerala has the highest sex ratio, with 1,084 females per 1,000 males.

question: Which one of the following statements is not correct? Dinar/New dinar is the currency of........... (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Sudan
option_2: Yugoslavia
option_3: UAE
option_4: Tunisia
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : 

The dinar (or New dinar) has been used as the currency in several countries — for example Yugoslavia used the dinar (and had issues with re-issued “new” dinars during monetary reforms), Tunisia uses the Tunisian dinar, and Sudan used a Sudanese dinar during the 1990s–2000s before returning to a pound. question: Indian Human Development Report does not give for each sample village............. (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: Infrastructure and Amenities Index
option_2: Education Related Index
option_3: Health Related Index
option_4: Unemployment Related Index
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (D) The Indian Human Development Report does not provide an unemployment-related index for each sample village. The report does provide indicators on income and livelihoods, but it does not specifically measure unemployment at the village level. The Human Development Index (HDI) : It is a statistical measure developed by the United Nations to evaluate the social and economic development of countries. The HDI considers three indicators of human development: 1. Life expectancy 2. Education 3. Per capita income. According to the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), India ranked 132nd out of 191 countries in the 2023 Human Development Index (HDI).

question: The Standing Committee of State Finance Ministers recommended in January 2000 uniform rates across the States in respect of............ (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: value-added tax
option_2: sales tax
option_3: stamp duty and registration fees
option_4: agricultural income-tax
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (B) In January 2000, the Standing Committee of State Finance Ministers recommended uniform sales tax rates across all states.  Sales tax : It is a tax paid to a governing body for the sale of certain goods and services. It’s a consumption tax charged at the point of purchase.  In 1999, steps were taken to rationalize the tax structure and remove distortions. It was decided to introduce a uniform State Level VAT. All states agreed to introduce VAT from April 1, 2005.  The Central Sales Tax (CST) was established to promote the smooth movement of goods across states by establishing a uniform tax structure throughout the country.  About GST: On 12 August 2016, Assam became the first state to ratify GST Bill. GST is a single tax on the supply of goods and services, right from the manufacturer to the consumer. Credits of input taxes paid at each stage will be available in the subsequent stage of value addition, which makes GST essentially a tax only on value addition at each stage. The GST Council is a constitutional body responsible for making recommendations on issues related to the implementation of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India.

question: Gilt-edged market means............ (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: bullion market
option_2: market of Government securities
option_3: market of guns
option_4: market of pure metals
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (B) The gilt-edged market is the market for government securities. The term “gilt-edged” refers to high-quality items that maintain a relatively constant value over time. Gilt-edged securities are high-grade bonds issued by some national governments. The government issues these securities, so they are considered risk-free. Gilt-edged securities only fail if the government experiences a financial crisis. The term “gilt-edged” can also refer to something that has gilded edges.

question: The Swarna Jayanti Shahari Rozgar Yojana which came into operation from Dec. 1, 1997 aims to provide gainful employment to the urban unemployed or underemployed poor but does not include............. (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: Nehru Rozgar Yojana
option_2: Urban Basic Services Programme
option_3: Prime Minister’s Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme
option_4: Prime Minister’s Rozgar Yojana
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (D) The Swarna Jayanti Shahari Rozgar Yojana (SJSRY) was launched in India on December 1, 1997. The scheme aims to provide employment and livelihood to the urban unemployed and underemployed poor. The SJSRY was implemented by the Ministry of Housing & Urban Poverty Alleviation. The SJSRY subsumed three earlier schemes for urban poverty alleviation: 1. Nehru Rozgar Yojana (NRY) 2. Urban Basic Services for the Poor (UBSP) 3. Prime Minister’s Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme (PMIUPEP).The SJSRY was replaced by the National Urban Livelihood Mission in 2013.

question: Resurgent India Bonds were issued in US Dollar, Pound Sterling and.............. (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: Japanese Yen
option_2: Deutsche Mark
option_3: Euro
option_4: French Franc
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (B) The Government of India issued Resurgent India Bonds (RIBs) in 1998 to attract foreign investors and Non-Resident Indians (NRIs). The bonds were issued in US dollars, pounds sterling, and Deutsche Marks. The bonds were bank instruments issued by the State Bank of India (SBI) that represented foreign currency denominated deposits. The bonds were offered to NRIs and overseas corporate bodies. The bonds had a five-year term and could be recouped entirely in dollars. In the UK, the bonds had a yield of 8 percent in pounds, while the average local return was between 6.5-7 percent. In Germany, NRIs were promised a return of 6.25 percent in DM. The Deutsche mark was Germany’s legal currency from 1948 to 2002. In 2002, Germany replaced the Deutsche mark with the euro.

question:
Consider the following statements: The Indian rupee is fully convertible............... (UPSC Prelims 2000)

1. in respect of Current Account of Balance of Payment.

2. in respect of Capital Account of Balance of Payment.

3. into gold.

Which of these statements is/are correct?
option_1: 1 alone
option_2: 3 alone
option_3: 1 and 2
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
EXPLANATION : (A) 

The Indian rupee is fully convertible only on the current account of the Balance of Payments. This means that individuals and businesses can freely exchange rupees for foreign currencies for trade in goods and services, travel, education, and remittances without restrictions. Thus, statement 1 is correct. The rupee is not fully convertible on the capital account of the Balance of Payments, as capital transactions such as investment, borrowing, and lending across borders are still controlled and monitored by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to prevent excessive volatility and protect the economy. Thus, statement 2 is incorrect. The rupee is not convertible into gold, as the system of gold convertibility ended globally after the collapse of the Bretton Woods system in the early 1970s. India, like most countries, does not back its currency with gold reserves. Thus, statement 3 is incorrect. Therefore, the correct answer is (A) 1 only.

question:

solution_text: EXPLANATION : (B) The Ministerial Meeting of the WTO in December 1999 was unsuccessful because it attempted to link trade with labor and environment related issues. The WTO’s Ministerial Conference is the highest body of the WTO and has the authority to make decisions under multilateral trade agreements. India has been a member of the World Trade Organization (WTO) since January 1, 1995.India participated in the 12th WTO Ministerial Conference (MC12) in Geneva, Switzerland in June 2022. T he 13th WTO Ministerial Conference took place from February 26–29, 2024 in Abu Dhabi.

question: A rise in ‘SENSEX’ means............. (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: a rise in prices of shares of all companies registered with Bombay Stock Exchange
option_2: a rise in prices of shares of all companies registered with National Stock Exchange
option_3: an overall rise in prices of shares of group of companies registered with Bombay Stock Exchange
option_4: a rise in prices of shares of all companies belonging to a group of companies registered with Bombay Stock Exchange.
solution_text: EXPLANATION : (C) A rise in the Sensex indicates an overall increase in the prices of the shares of the companies included in the index. The Sensex is a benchmark index of the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) in India. It is made up of 30 of the largest and most actively traded stocks on the BSE. The Sensex acts as a proxy for the Indian stock markets. It is reviewed semiannually each year in June and December. The term Sensex was coined by Deepak Mohoni, a stock market analyst in 1989. The full form of Sensex is Stock Exchange Sensitive Index.
